sentences.text: For the Russians, yes, because they supplied, you know, food for the hospital.
And I remember the cook there, he cooked something with condensed milk, those tins of condensed milk.
And half of them they left in.
And we thought, oh, you know, that's a lot in.
We'll take them.
But we were scared.
But, but after all, they throw it out.
They wouldn't do anything.
But we thought we were stealing.
We were so scared [INAUDIBLE] because what we went through with the Germans.
And we took a whole lot of the tins, you know, we took them home.
And there was a little boy before he went to the hospital, and we spooned it out.
There was just a lot of the thick milk in.
It was lovely.
I don't know.
For a purpose, I suppose.
I've got six children.
Must be, because my husband survived.
I don't know why.
Well, then--
Not very long.
Then the Americans came and put us in trucks to go to Leipzig to the American zone.
And they took me straight away to sit in the front, you know.
I was a young girl.
I got chocolates.
[LAUGHS] It was lovely.
We stayed a couple of days in Leipzig.
And then trucks came again and took us to the slums of Holland, not Amsterdam straight away.
We stayed there a few days, and you know, we got, you know, plenty to eat.
And Friday night we were invited to some people, because the south of Holland was liberated long before the north.
So there were kind of settled already.
And I never forget those people, you know.
They gave us a lovely night.
And they said now we've got friends in Amsterdam.
I'll give you the address, and when you-- I'm ashamed to say that-- and when you're in Amsterdam, you'll give them the parcel with cheese in it.
Do you think the cheese came to those people?
We ate it.
[LAUGHS] Fancy doing that after people coming from the camp.
Yeah.
That's what happened, yes.
Then after a few days, trucks came again and we went to Amsterdam.
And we went to the central station.
It was the receiving center.
And where the registration, you know, different departments-- police, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] distribution, anything, you know-- all what you need for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] registration.
And there was the Jewish authorities.
When I said my name, they said, you're the fiancee of Nick Melkman.
And he's here too.
He comes every day to see you.
He saw you on the list.
Just now he's not here.
They gave me his address.
And when I was put in a-- what used to be an orphanage-- and as soon as I came there, I said, can I use your phone?
And they thought I was mad because, you know, who uses the phone straight after the war?
They had to be very economical.
When I told them why, I could.
And I rang the home where he was, and he wasn't there.
I left a message.
And I went upstairs in the ward with about five people.
And well, [INAUDIBLE].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Of course, you know, we all had the runs, you know, we had to go to toilet.
And I was just trying to find clean clothes for myself.
And there was a knock at the door.
And it was one of the servants and said, he is there.
He's downstairs.
So I had no undies on.
[LAUGHS] And I went downstairs, running downstairs, the last steps I couldn't, you know, and he was waiting downstairs.
I said, what's that?
And he had a, a scar here.
It was one of the kapos.
He said I was in the camp, in the first five minutes, I was in his way.
And he copped it.
And I said, your ring?
And he said, I lost it.
I said, so did I.
[LAUGHS]
When we were in Trebitz, I kept it all the time.
But I lost so much weight that one morning I went to the toilet and I dropped it in.
It fell off my finger.
[LAUGHS]
No.
Some papers.
Yeah, some papers.
Like, like Betty's birth certificate, and things like that, you know.
[INAUDIBLE]
No.
No, no.
So that night we went straight away to see those people, you know, who got these letters.
We didn't even talk about that, did we?
You don't know about that?
Yeah.
And they had some clothes for us.
My mother gave, you know, at the time, if anyone comes back, here's some underwear.
If they're badly needed, it was there.
And yeah.
Well, when my husband, he went to a camp, you know, after he was picked up in the street.
And he went to the prison and travelled with my parents.
And he came in a camp, Seibersdorf, which was not quite finished.
And it was a railway going.
And he saw that had coal trains going to Trieste in Italy.
And he thought, if I can get on a train like that, you know?
And at the same time, there was somebody that said, hey Nick, what about escaping?
And Nick said, tonight, straight away.
So somehow they climbed the fence and they came to the railway line.
And I said it was arranged.
The train came, and they climbed in the brake compartment, and they travelled and travelled until they came near-- I can't remember where.
But the train stopped, and they were caught.
And they said, well, we had a bombardment in Stettin, and our names are so-and-so-- different names.
We're scared for the bombs, and we went to get away from it.
So they took them to Munich and courthouse, and they were sent to the-- how they call it here?
The work, S, to get work, the Social Security.
Alright.
The Social Security.
And he got a job at a butcher, and the other boy got another job somewhere else.
And--
Oh, OK.
But while he was free, he sent a letter to friends of mine, and I was still at home and I got that letter.
And we sent him clothes, be he never got it, because they had to go through it again.
Anyhow, to make things short, he was caught again at the Swiss border and sent to prison, to Innsbruck, where he stayed a year.
He put on weight there.
He had a good time there working in library.
And then they couldn't find out who he was.
Then investigated in Holland those people who got the letter.
And they said, do you know Hank [?
Fissel? ?] And the man said, yes, I know Hank [?
Fissel. ?] I used to work with him on the docks.
And he's a tall fellow with grey hair.
So--
Yes, yes.
Yes, that's why they went to the address of that man.
Oh, hidden.
Was is all right, because he was in prison.
I'm all right here.
There's some people who try to get to the Swiss front here, but I wouldn't do a thing like that.
You know, like that.
[SIGHS]
Yes, like guarded.
Guarded, yes.
Well, in the beginning, when I was home.
But later on they had to keep 'em.
And they had underwear that my mother's gave.
[INAUDIBLE] Before my mother went, she said, you take this and take that.
If anyone comes back, you know, give them.
At least I've got something to wear.
Which was, you know, Godsent.
Godsent to have anything.
Underwear mainly, mainly, and all the photos.
Lots of photos.
And a few things, but they kept.
Yeah.
[SIGHS] Then there was not much for us to do in Holland.
We did-- my husband used to be in cattle transport.
And when he saw big trucks with the names on it, and there used to be anti-Jewish, they threw him out of a job.
And they had the big jobs now.
He didn't feel like staying there.
And in the meantime, there were posters everywhere.
Join the Marines, you take your training in America.
So he joined the Marines.
And then when the war with Japan was finished, he got the training in-- in Holland to be sent to Indonesia, to Dutch East Indies, it was.
I went to England.
Some friends [? wanted ?] me there.
When did you go to England?
In about November of that year, '45.
He went into training, I went to England.
And then in February, '46, he came over and we got married.
It was good.
You know, I was a good, you know, girl, you know, a young girl from the camps, you know.
I had a fantastic time.
I was-- you know, they had two daughters where I was.
And I'm still friendly with them.
They're like my sisters.
Nothing.
Just-- even the daughters didn't do anything to help the mother in the house.
And they clothed me.
And, you know, they gave me the wedding-- 300 people at the wedding, I didn't know anyone.
[LAUGHS]
It was happy and it was sad, you know.
No parents there.
Nobody I knew.
But they gave me a fantastic wedding.
Then he had two weeks leave, then he left.
And--
Yeah.
Then he came back after, after two weeks, I think, on embarkation leave.
[INAUDIBLE] So we had another two weeks together.
And then we said goodbye.
And one morning, early in the morning was a telegram.
Come to Southampton, because there were boat docks there.
So quickly borrowed some money, got dressed, went to the station and to Southampton.
About seven hours in the train.
Found a boat at night in the dark.
And we had another couple of days in London.
Yeah.
And that was it.
Then he went to Indonesia, and about a year later I followed him.
Nothing.
Just, you know, helped the girls, went shopping with the girls.
They did the shopping for the mother.
And [SIGHS] yeah.
Yeah, from the Red Cross, that [INAUDIBLE] they were killed.
The young brother.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Grandmothers, yeah, yeah.
Happy and not happy.
If I wouldn't have found Nick back, maybe I would have felt the loss of the parents more.
But I was, it overruled, kind of, the happiness to find him back again.
No, because it was one good thing, and that was it.
After all, that was future.
In Indonesia.
In December, December '46.
I was married in February, December, '46.
It was lovely.
Lovely at went there, but then my husband got sick.
He got pneumonia and emphysema, and he was seven months in the hospital there.
And I had two children then by that time.
And he was put on a machine that pumped of puss, you know, that's in his [? lungs. ?] That's not water on, on the lungs, but puss.
And bottles and bottles of puss.
Anyhow--
An infection on the lungs.
Probably, yes.
Then he started getting fits.
He had fits, too, because of that, that cut he had.
So he was not a healthy man after the war.
Anyhow, he was dismissed from hospital and dismissed from, from the Marines, and sent back to Holland.
There was a little bit of the stuff back in-- left in this lungs.
They said it would disappear on its own.
And-- and when we came in Holland, it started again.
We didn't have a house to go to, but the Marines put us in a hotel from the Salvation Army.
And there he got sick again.
And he spent another eight months in hospital, got a lung operation.
It was terrible, because in the meantime, we got a house allocated to us.
First of all, we refused it, because it was in an attic, a refurbished attic, which we refused.
Then we got a house allocated.
He was in hospital.
I had to clean it.
I had to buy anything, from a teaspoon to curtains to bed, everything.
So what did I do?
I put the children in a private nursery.
So I had my hands free and could do whatever.
And then finally I had it all going.
The house was all ready, warmed up, I had a playpen for the youngest, everything.
I can't pick up the children.
I can't get my daughter because she had a mid-ear infection.
I had to wait a few days.
Got her, finally.
Then I travel to the hospital all the time, because it was out of Amsterdam.
Finally he's improved, he can come home.
The children had whooping cough, he can't come home.
[LAUGHS] But all ends well.
All's well, ends well.
[INAUDIBLE] '50, I think, in 1950.
And then, of course, he didn't have a job.
He didn't like Holland anymore.
And we decided, his brother was here already, we decided to go to Australia.
Don't know what to expect from Australia.
Never a word of Australia.
His brother was here, we go.
[LAUGHS] Didn't know anything.
No, no.
Because we were together.
We had a family and that was it.
Two, and then got four more here.
That was terrible, too, because we paid for our own passage.
Not my husband, because he was an ex-militaire.
The agency, the travel agency promised us-- it was a new boat, the maiden voyage-- you go to Genoa on the train, and there will be sleepers.
And everything was beautiful.
There were no sleepers, nothing.
We came on the boat--
Oceania.
It was a brand new Italian boat.
In Sicily, we picked up Italians, migrants.
There were two sittings at the table.
The first one spilled all the wine over the table, the second one would sit there.
People-- women were feeding their babies at the table.
Uh, we had such a terrible trip.
Nobody to look after the children.
And we thought it would be beautiful on a maiden voyage.
But never mind.
We came in Melbourne.
[LAUGHS] There was a telegram--you wouldn't believe that-- a telegram from his brother.
Please bring butter, as much as you can.
Because there was a shortage of butter.
And we couldn't understand.
If there's no butter in Sydney, why should we bring it from Melbourne?
[LAUGHS] We couldn't understand that.
So we bought a whole box of butter.
Because his father-in-law had a shop, and we bought a whole box of butter.
[LAUGHS] His father-in-law?
Yeah.
Oh, he got married in the meantime, because he was a Marine, too.
And he had correspondence with that girl, and they sponsored him.
And as soon as he came to Australia, he married that girl.
We went straightaway to my brother-in-law's house, because we brought our own home with us to be built here, prefabricated, but it hadn't arrived yet.
So for the time being, we were with the in-laws.
But he already bought a block of land in Loftus, in the southern interior, because that was an area where you were allowed prefabricated houses.
Yeah.
My husband found, straightaway, work in a store of a big-- it doesn't exist anymore, [INAUDIBLE], used to be a big grocery chain.
And from then on, he had so many jobs.
But his last job was for about 20 years, he worked in a Sutherland hospital.
Yes, he worked for Parke-Davis.
See, first of all, he worked in the city.
And then in the Sutherland Shire they started to open up, you know, different opportunities.
He worked for Parke-Davis, for Nemco, which was furniture.
Pick and Shuffle, he did.
Anything he could lay his hands on.
His health?
Well, no good, because he had those fits.
Small ones, in the beginning.
I noticed that in Indonesia, in his sleep, he used to do, like tics.
And I thought he was joking, you know.
But I couldn't wake him up from then on, you know.
But at the end it wasn't that bad.
He only had little attacks and had it under control.
OK.
Six.
I've got Herman.
He'll be here later.
He's the oldest.
Sarah, that's my daughter.
She might be here.
Then I had David and Lester and Louis, he'll be here.
Louis, and Jacqueline.
Well, Australia really itself hasn't done much, but being here-- I need them, let me put it that way.
I didn't need Australia to do things for me.
But being here and being free and having the children, it's a good country.
Let me put it that way.
Well, the last year I start to feel my age a little bit, but it's not nothing serious.
Not really, because I'm 75.
And you know, little, little, little complaints, not serious.
Yes, I've had one operation on the, on the buttock.
I had a cyst there.
And the doctors reckon it was a dirty needle.
I said, well, that's possible, because when I had the typhoid, I had plenty of needles.
Yes, I had the operation '50, in about '55, 1956.
It was a cyst on the buttock.
What struck me was the old cars you saw.
And I had beautiful clothes with me for the children.
You didn't see any nice clothes here.
They were very far behind, I reckon, with everything.
I, I remember on, near Museum Station, you know, they had the police in a high, like in a-- like a house on stilts, that regulating the traffic.
There were no traffic lights.
That's the first thing we noticed.
And we've come a long way.
Because I'll tell you, they still don't think much of Australia overseas.
Because I had a letter from a cousin the other day, and she said-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]
No.
No.
Yes.
I had them spread out very well, because I had the first two just a year apart.
And then after four years, I had two with 18 months space.
And then another four years with 18 months space.
Not, it wasn't broken up like that, but it just happened.
Which was good, because I never had more than two at home.
Then went to school.
And it was lovely for them to grow up where we lived in the Sutherland Shire.
It was very quiet.
No traffic in the street.
They could go all day, playing outside.
My husband was a big help when he was home.
He would always help out.
When baby was born, he saw to it that he had his holidays.
He was a good supplier and a good help.
And I still miss him after 10 years.
Yeah, we think about it, yeah.
We still think about it.
Yes.
If I wake up in the morning at 6 o'clock, I can't sleep anymore, thinking about everything, memories.
Sometimes.
I see people who are dead.
What can you do?
What can you do?
There's nothing you can do.
For me?
Lost years.
Really, lost years.
I-- you probably have contact with girlfriends, school friends, you might have a school reunion.
I can't talk with no one about my early days.
There's no one there, no friends, no school friends, nobody.
It's, it's a real miss.
Even from when I was a teenager and went to work, all those friends, they're all gone, too.
So that's an, an aftereffect very much.
That's terrible too, yes.
Yeah.
Hate the Germans, hate them.
Don't trust them.
Yup.
Yup.
Because people say, yeah, but it's a new generation, they're different.
That generation, God knows what they would have done if they would have won the war.
And our generation, same generation isn't there anymore.
So--
No.
Not forgive and not forget.
No, can't.
Wouldn't you if they murdered your parents?
Somebody murdered your parents?
What would you do?
I don't think so.
I hope I've, you know, helped out.
I think so, yeah.
Yeah.
OK.
